g.data-narration: Welcome to this lecture on Multicast Routing and MOSPF, an essential protocol for efficient network communication.
Multicast routing delivers data from one source to multiple receivers simultaneously, optimizing bandwidth by replicating packets only at network branches.
The multicast group address range is 224.0.0.0 to 239.255.255.255 (Class D). Three main components work together: source, group members, and routers.
IGMP manages host membership in multicast groups at the local network. Hosts send join/leave messages, and routers query to discover group members.
g.text: Manages host membership at local network segment (Layer 2/3 boundary)

g.data-narration: MOSPF (Multicast OSPF) extends OSPF to support IP multicast routing. Introduced in RFC 1584, it uses OSPF's link-state database for multicast paths.
g.text: MOSPF - Multicast Open Shortest Path First
Extension of OSPF (RFC 1584, 1994) for IP multicast routing within autonomous systems
Uses Dijkstra's algorithm for SPT computation

g.data-narration: MOSPF operates in three modes: intra-area (within one OSPF area), inter-area (between areas), and inter-AS (between autonomous systems).
MOSPF introduces group-membership LSA (Type 6) that advertises which multicast groups have members on which networks, flooding throughout the OSPF area.
g.text: Advertises which multicast groups have members on which networks
All routers build complete membership topology

g.data-narration: MOSPF defines specialized routers: ABRs connect areas, ASBRs connect to external networks, IAMFs forward between areas, and ISMFs handle inter-AS forwarding.
MOSPF computes source-specific shortest path trees for each (source, group) pair using Dijkstra's algorithm, determining optimal forwarding paths.
g.text: Two-phase approach: (1) Determine if router lies on SPT, (2) Determine forwarding interfaces
Use Dijkstra's algorithm with group membership info
Determine which interfaces should forward the packet
For each (Source, Group) pair → Compute separate SPT

g.data-narration: Consider four routers: R1 connects to source S, R2 and R3 have group members, R4 is a transit router. Link costs shown determine optimal paths.
A router determines upstream interface toward source and downstream interfaces to group members. It maintains (S, G) state for forwarding decisions.
For inter-area multicast, ABRs (IAMFs) forward group membership information between OSPF areas, enabling multicast to cross area boundaries.
g.text: R2 generates Type 6 LSA → R1 learns membership → R1 forwards to R2

g.data-narration: Compare multicast protocols: DVMRP uses distance-vector, PIM is most widely deployed, CBT uses shared trees, and MOSPF extends OSPF for seamless integration.
Key takeaways: MOSPF extends OSPF for multicast using Type 6 LSAs, computes source-specific SPTs dynamically, and operates in three modes within an AS.
g.text: MOSPF = OSPF + Multicast | Type 6 LSA for group membership | Dijkstra for SPT | Three modes: Intra/Inter-area/Inter-AS
